panel.origin: Tennis term for a score of zero. Used throughout a game — '15-love', 'love-30', and so on.
panel.mixology: She held to love, winning all four points without dropping a single one — a flawless service game.
panel.examples: Most likely from the phrase 'to play for love' (for nothing, no stakes); a popular but disputed theory traces it to the French 'l'œuf' (egg, for the zero's shape).

panel.origin: The score when both players reach 40-40 (three points each). From deuce, a player must win two points in a row to take the game.
panel.mixology: The game reached deuce again and again, neither able to win the two straight points needed to close it out.
panel.examples: From the French 'à deux' (at two) — meaning two points are needed to win — via the Old French 'deus' (two).

panel.origin: The point won immediately after deuce. If the same player wins the next point, they take the game; if not, the score returns to deuce.
panel.mixology: He won the deuce point for advantage, then sealed the game on the very next rally.
panel.examples: From the Old French 'avantage' (a favorable position) — the player who scores from deuce holds the upper hand, one point from the game.

panel.origin: The phrase announcing the end of a match: a 'game' is the first unit, a 'set' is a group of games, and 'match' is the whole contest, decided by sets.
panel.mixology: With one final ace, the umpire called 'Game, set, and match' — the contest was over.
panel.examples: Each term nests inside the next: points build a game, games build a set, sets build the match — the tiered structure of tennis scoring stated in order.

panel.origin: A special game played at 6-6 in a set to decide the winner, first to 7 points (win by two), avoiding endlessly long sets.
panel.mixology: Locked at 6-6, they went to a tiebreak, and she edged it 7-5 to take the set.
panel.examples: From the plain sense of 'breaking' a tie. Introduced in the 1970s (credited to Jimmy Van Alen) to keep sets from running on indefinitely.

panel.origin: A point that, if won by the returner, would win the game against the server. A pivotal moment, since holding serve is the norm.
panel.mixology: She earned break point at 30-40 and ripped a return winner to steal his service game.
panel.examples: Descriptive: winning it 'breaks' the server's expected hold of their own game — among the highest-pressure points in tennis.

panel.origin: To 'hold' is to win your own service game; to 'break' is to win the game when your opponent is serving. The basic rhythm of a set.
panel.mixology: Both players held serve comfortably until she finally earned the break to go up 5-4.
panel.examples: From 'holding' onto the advantage the server is expected to keep; 'break' from breaking that expected pattern of service dominance.

panel.origin: Slang for winning a set 6-0. The zero resembles a bagel. A 'double bagel' is a 6-0, 6-0 win.
panel.mixology: She handed out a bagel in the first set, racing through it 6-0 before her opponent found any rhythm.
panel.examples: From the round, zero-shaped bagel standing in for the '0' in a 6-0 set — a piece of affectionate locker-room slang.

panel.origin: A stroke hit with the palm-side of the hand facing the direction of the swing — for most players, the more natural and powerful side.
panel.mixology: He ran around his backhand to crush an inside-out forehand, his biggest weapon, into the open court.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'fore' (front) of the hand leads the stroke, as opposed to the back of the hand on a backhand.

panel.origin: A stroke hit with the back of the hand facing the swing's direction, played across the body. Can be one- or two-handed.
panel.mixology: Her two-handed backhand down the line was a thing of beauty, flat and deep into the corner.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'back' of the hand leads the stroke. The one-handed backhand is the classic form; the two-hander rose to dominance in the modern game.

panel.origin: A shot struck before the ball bounces, usually played near the net to cut off the rally and finish the point quickly.
panel.mixology: He charged the net and put away a crisp volley into the open court before she could recover.
panel.examples: From the French 'volée' (a flight), via 'voler' (to fly) — the ball is struck while still in flight, before touching the ground.

panel.origin: A shot hit after the ball has bounced once, from the back of the court. Forehands and backhands from the baseline are groundstrokes.
panel.mixology: The two traded heavy groundstrokes from the baseline, neither giving an inch in the long exchange.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the ball is struck off the 'ground' after its bounce, distinguishing it from a volley taken out of the air.

panel.origin: A shot hit high over an opponent who is at the net, sending the ball deep behind them to reset or win the point.
panel.mixology: Stranded at the net, he could only watch as her perfect lob floated over his head and dropped on the baseline.
panel.examples: From the old sense of 'lob' (to throw or hit in a high arc) — a soft, rising trajectory designed to clear the opponent.

panel.origin: A powerful overhead shot, hit like a serve, to put away a high ball — often the answer to a lob. Also called an 'overhead'.
panel.mixology: She read the weak lob early and crushed an overhead smash that bounced into the stands.
panel.examples: From 'to smash' (to strike with great force). The overhead motion mirrors the serve and delivers one of the game's most forceful shots.

panel.origin: A delicately struck shot that barely clears the net and dies quickly, used to catch a deep opponent off guard.
panel.mixology: With his rival pinned behind the baseline, he feathered a drop shot that two bounces couldn't save.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the ball 'drops' just over the net with heavy backspin and minimal pace — a finesse shot, the opposite of power.

panel.origin: A shot hit with backspin by drawing the racket down and through the ball, producing a low, skidding bounce. Common on the backhand.
panel.mixology: She mixed in a low backhand slice that skidded and stayed low, disrupting his rhythm completely.
panel.examples: From the cutting, downward 'slicing' motion across the ball that imparts the backspin — the same root as the golf slice's glancing contact.

panel.origin: A serve that the receiver fails to touch at all, winning the point outright. The server's ultimate weapon.
panel.mixology: Down break point, he fired an ace down the T at 130 mph to erase the danger instantly.
panel.examples: From the playing-card 'ace' (the single highest card) via its general sense of 'the best' — an unreturned serve being the perfect outcome.

panel.origin: A serve that fails — landing out, in the net, or with a foot fault. Each point allows two serve attempts before a fault costs the point.
panel.mixology: His first serve clipped the net cord and dropped wide — a fault — leaving him a second serve to play.
panel.examples: From the general sense of 'fault' (an error or failing) — the serve fails to meet the rules, an unforced mistake on the most controlled shot.

panel.origin: Missing both serve attempts on a point, which hands the point directly to the opponent. A costly, self-inflicted error.
panel.mixology: Serving for the set, she tightened up and double-faulted, gifting her opponent the crucial point.
panel.examples: Descriptive: two consecutive 'faults' (failed serves) in a row. Among the most demoralizing ways to lose a point in tennis.

panel.origin: A serve that clips the top of the net but still lands in the correct service box — it's replayed with no penalty. Also any point ordered to be replayed.
panel.mixology: His serve ticked the net cord and dribbled in; the umpire called a let, and he served again.
panel.examples: From an archaic sense of 'let' meaning to hinder or obstruct (as in 'without let or hindrance') — the net 'lets' or interferes with the serve.

panel.origin: Each point gives the server two tries: the 'first serve' is hit harder and riskier; the safer 'second serve' must go in or it's a double fault.
panel.mixology: He missed a big first serve, then spun in a cautious second serve to start the rally safely.
panel.examples: Descriptive of the two-attempt structure: the first is the aggressive bid, the second a controlled fallback to avoid the double fault.

panel.origin: A serve hit with heavy topspin that bounces high and jumps to the side, hard to attack. A reliable, safe second serve.
panel.mixology: Her kick serve leapt up above his shoulder, forcing a weak return she pounced on.
panel.examples: From the way the heavy topspin makes the ball 'kick' upward and sideways off the bounce — a high, awkward bounce rather than a flat skid.

panel.origin: The line at the back of the court marking the boundary for play and the spot from which serves are struck. Baseliners play mostly from here.
panel.mixology: A classic baseliner, she camped behind the baseline and dictated play with deep, heavy groundstrokes.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'base' line forming the rearmost boundary of each side of the court — the foundation line of the playing area.

panel.origin: The barrier dividing the court in half, which the ball must pass over. Also the act of 'coming to net' to volley.
panel.mixology: He followed his approach to the net, looking to finish with a volley before she could pass him.
panel.examples: From the literal mesh net strung across the court's center — both the physical object and, by extension, the forecourt area beside it.

panel.origin: The two halves of each baseline: the 'deuce court' is the right side (where the deuce point is served), the 'ad court' the left (where advantage points are served).
panel.mixology: On break point in the ad court, she served wide to pull him off the deuce side entirely.
panel.examples: Named for the score served to each side: 'deuce' points are played to the right court, 'advantage' (ad) points to the left.

panel.origin: The narrow lanes on each side of the court, in play only for doubles. Also called the 'tramlines'. A singles ball landing here is out.
panel.mixology: His passing shot caught the doubles alley by an inch — in for doubles, but it would've been out in singles.
panel.examples: 'Alley' from the narrow-lane sense; 'tramlines' (British) from the resemblance to the parallel rails of a tram track running down each side.

panel.origin: The marked rectangles into which a serve must land to be legal — one on each side, diagonally across from the server.
panel.mixology: Her serve clipped the corner of the service box, perfectly placed and impossible to read.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the boxed area designated to receive the 'service' (serve). A serve missing the box is a fault.

panel.origin: An extended exchange of shots between players after the serve is returned, lasting until the point ends.
panel.mixology: They engaged in a 30-shot rally, each chasing down impossible balls until she finally cracked a winner.
panel.examples: From the older sense of 'rally' (to come back together, regroup) — the back-and-forth gathering of shot after shot in a single point.

panel.origin: A shot hit while moving forward toward the net, designed to set up a winning volley by forcing a weak reply.
panel.mixology: He drove a deep approach shot to her backhand and followed it in, ready to volley the floating return.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the shot played as the player 'approaches' the net, transitioning from baseline to forecourt to finish the point.

panel.origin: A mistake made entirely under the player's own control, not forced by a good shot from the opponent. A key stat for measuring sloppy play.
panel.mixology: She lost the set on unforced errors, spraying easy balls long and wide rather than being beaten outright.
panel.examples: Descriptive: an error 'unforced' by the opponent's pressure — a self-inflicted miss, contrasted with a 'forced error' caused by a tough shot.

panel.origin: A shot the opponent can't touch, ending the point cleanly in the hitter's favor — the positive counterpart to an unforced error.
panel.mixology: He ended the marathon rally with a forehand winner down the line, clean and untouchable.
panel.examples: From the plain sense of the shot that 'wins' the point outright — struck beyond the reach of the opponent.

panel.origin: To win the opponent's service game immediately after losing your own. A momentum-swinging recovery.
panel.mixology: Right after dropping serve, she broke back to love, erasing the deficit and steadying the set.
panel.examples: Descriptive: regaining ('breaking back') the service break you just surrendered — answering a break with a break of your own.

panel.origin: Winning all four major championships (Australian, French, Wimbledon, US Open) in a single year. Also used loosely for the majors themselves.
panel.mixology: Holding three majors, she chased the calendar Grand Slam at the US Open under enormous pressure.
panel.examples: Borrowed from the card game bridge (winning all the tricks) and earlier from golf, applied to sweeping tennis's four biggest titles.

panel.origin: The electronic ball-tracking system used to review whether a ball landed in or out. Players issue a 'challenge' to trigger a review.
panel.mixology: He challenged the call, and Hawk-Eye showed the ball had clipped the line by a millimeter — point reversed.
panel.examples: Named for the brand of the tracking technology, evoking a hawk's sharp vision; the player's request to use it is called a 'challenge'.

panel.origin: A very high, slow, looping groundstroke that lands deep, used to disrupt an opponent's rhythm and buy time. Often a defensive or frustrating tactic.
panel.mixology: Pushed off the court, she threw up a moonball to reset the point and scramble back into position.
panel.examples: A coinage evoking a ball lofted so high it seems to head for the moon — slow, towering, and deliberately rhythm-breaking.
